focus: Flap T and Flap D — American 'Tapped' Consonants Between Vowels
focus_ar: صوت الفلاب T و D — الصوت الأمريكي 'المطروق' بين حرفين متحركين
arabic_challenge: Arabic /t/ and /d/ are always pronounced fully and clearly, wherever they appear. In American English, when /t/ or /d/ falls between two vowel sounds (and the second vowel is unstressed), it becomes a quick tongue-tap called a flap [ɾ] — very close to a Spanish/Arabic light 'r' sound. Saying a full, hard /t/ in 'water' or 'butter' is one of the clearest markers of a non-native American accent.

isolation.instructions: When /t/ sits between two vowels and the syllable after it is unstressed, American speakers tap the tongue tip quickly against the ridge — much softer and faster than a full /t/. It sounds almost like a fast /d/ or light Arabic ر.
isolation.instructions_ar: لما /t/ تقع بين حرفين متحركين والمقطع اللي بعدها بدون نبر، المتكلم الأمريكي بيطرق طرف لسانه بسرعة — صوت أخف وأسرع من /t/ الكاملة. قريب من صوت راء عربي خفيف.

sentence_practice: I need a little water for the party.
That's better butter than the city store has.
It doesn't matter — she's a pretty writer.
daily_drills.record_this: I bought some butter and water at the party in the city — it's a pretty little place.
daily_drills.self_check: Say 'water' twice: once with a hard, clear /t/, once flapped. The flapped version should feel faster and softer — almost lazy. That lazy version is the natural American sound.

isolation.instructions: The same flap happens with /d/ between vowels — it merges with the /t/ flap sound, which is why 'writer' and 'rider' can sound almost identical in fast American speech (the vowel length is the only real difference).
isolation.instructions_ar: نفس صوت الفلاب بيحصل مع /d/ بين حرفين متحركين — بيتشابه مع فلاب /t/، وده سبب إن writer و rider ممكن يتشابهوا في الكلام السريع (طول حرف العلة هو الفرق الحقيقي).

pair: *mutty (not a real word — used to feel the contrast)

sentence_practice: My buddy is ready for the wedding.
The ladder was muddy after I climbed it.
I study every day until my shoulder hurts.
daily_drills.record_this: My buddy is ready to study for the wedding — his body is muddy from climbing that old ladder.
daily_drills.self_check: Are you tapping your tongue quickly for both /t/ and /d/ in these words, rather than making a hard stop? If it sounds soft and fast, you've got it.

isolation.instructions: The flap doesn't only happen inside one word — it also happens when a word ending in /t/ or /d/ is followed by a word starting with a vowel, in connected speech.
isolation.instructions_ar: الفلاب ما بيحصلش فقط داخل كلمة واحدة — بيحصل كمان لما كلمة تنتهي بـ /t/ أو /d/ وبعدها كلمة تبدأ بحرف متحرك، في الكلام المتصل.

sentence_practice: What about getting up early to put it away?
There's a lot of work — not at all easy today.
Get up! We're going to be late.
daily_drills.record_this: What about getting up early? There's a lot of work, and not at all of it is easy — but get up anyway.
daily_drills.self_check: Do 'get up' and 'put it' flow as ONE smooth sound rather than two separate, choppy words?

target_sounds: contrast: flap T vs. glottal stop preview (before nasal N)

isolation.instructions: Not every /t/ becomes a flap. Before a syllabic /n/ (like in 'button' or 'mountain'), Americans usually use a GLOTTAL STOP instead — a brief closure in the throat — which you'll study in depth next week. Today, just notice the difference: flap = tongue tap; glottal stop = throat catch.
isolation.instructions_ar: ماكل /t/ بتتحول لفلاب. قبل صوت /n/ المقطعي (زي في button أو mountain)، الأمريكان بيستخدموا وقفة حنجرية بديل — قفلة سريعة في الحلق — هتدرسها بالتفصيل الأسبوع الجاي. النهارده بس لاحظ الفرق.

sentence_practice: Press the button for more water.
The little kitten climbed the mountain.
It's better, no matter what's written.
daily_drills.record_this: Press the button, drink some water, and climb the little mountain — no matter what is written, it's better than staying home.
daily_drills.self_check: Can you feel your throat catch briefly for 'button' and 'mountain', while your tongue just taps lightly for 'water' and 'better'? These are two different mechanisms — don't mix them up.

target_sounds: flap T/D in fast, natural sentences

isolation.instructions: Today, focus on speed. The flap only sounds natural at normal-to-fast conversational speed. Practice building up from slow to natural pace without losing the flap.
isolation.instructions_ar: النهارده الفوكس على السرعة. الفلاب بيبان طبيعي بس في سرعة كلام عادية لسريعة. تدرب من البطيء للسريع بدون ما تخسر صوت الفلاب.

pair: a lot of water (fast, flapped, connected)

sentence_practice: I need a little bit of water, and it doesn't matter what city.
She's a better writer than her buddy, no matter what.
Study a little every day, and it gets a lot better.
daily_drills.record_this: It doesn't matter what city — a little water and butter make everything better, and studying a little every day gets you a lot further.
daily_drills.self_check: Record this sentence 3 times, each faster than the last. Did the flap survive at full speed, or did it turn back into a hard /t/ under pressure? Keep practicing until it holds.

daily_drills.target_sounds: Review: flap T, flap D, cross-word flapping, flap vs. glottal-stop contrast, natural speed
challenge_sentences: My buddy the writer is ready to study a little better every day.
Get up and put a lot of water and butter on the table in the city.
It doesn't matter — the ladder is muddy, but it's better than the mountain.
daily_drills.record_this: Every day my buddy studies a little better. He's a writer who likes to get up early, put water and butter on the table, and climb a muddy ladder — better than the mountain, no matter what.
daily_drills.self_check: Listen back for every flapped /t/ and /d/ — count them. This week's passage should have at least 10. Did you flap most of them naturally?

test_yourself.instructions: Record this passage at a natural, comfortable conversational speed — not slow, not rushed.
test_yourself.passage: Every Saturday, my buddy and I get up early and go to the city to study at a little cafe. The water there is better than at home, and the butter on the bread is amazing. Last week, a writer sat next to us — he was working on a book about a mountain village. It doesn't matter what day it is; a little coffee and a lot of conversation always make it a good morning.

sounds_to_check.flap T (between vowels): water, better, little, city, matter, writer

scoring_guide.90_100: Flap consistently used between vowels and across word boundaries at natural speed; glottal stop correctly used for 'mountain' instead.
scoring_guide.70_89: Flap present most of the time; occasional hard /t/ under stress or at slower speed.
scoring_guide.50_69: Flap inconsistent — present in isolated word drills but lost in full sentences.
scoring_guide.below_50: Full, hard /t/ and /d/ throughout — go back and repeat Days 1-5, focusing on speed.

for_arabic_speakers: The flap sound is genuinely close to a light Arabic ر — use that similarity as a shortcut rather than trying to learn a totally foreign sound.
This is one of the single highest-impact accent features for sounding natural in American English. Prioritize it even over some vowel work if you're short on time this week.
Do not flap /t/ or /d/ at the START of a word or a stressed syllable ('table', 'today') — only between vowels where the SECOND vowel is unstressed.
supplementary_notes.recording_requirement: Record the 'record_this' sentence every day. By Day 7, your flaps should sound automatic, not deliberate.
